1: wayyiqrĕḇû yĕmê-Dāwîḏ lāmûṯ wayyĕṣaw ʾeṯ-šĕlōmōh bĕnô: 'The days of David drew near that he should die, and he charged his son Solomon.' The phrase wayyiqrĕḇû yĕmê X lāmûṯ (the days of X drew near to die) is the standard deathbed narrative opening (Genesis 47:29: Jacob; Deuteronomy 31:14: Moses). David's charge to Solomon is the royal testament — the dying father's final counsel to his successor, parallel in structure to Moses's farewell speeches (Deut 31-33), Jacob's deathbed blessings (Gen 49), and Joshua's farewell (Josh 23-24). The formal genre signals the ideological importance of what follows.
2: ʾānōḵî hōlēḵ bĕḏereḵ kol-hāʾāreṣ wĕḥāzaqtā wĕhāyîṯā lĕʾîš: 'I am going the way of all the earth. Be strong and show yourself a man.' The phrase ḏereḵ kol-hāʾāreṣ (the way of all the earth) is a Hebrew euphemism for death — all earthly beings travel this path. The commission formula ḥāzaq wihyēh lĕʾîš (be strong and be a man/show yourself a man) echoes the standard commissioning language: YHWH to Joshua (Joshua 1:6, 7, 9: ḥāzaq weʾĕmāṣ), David to Solomon in 1 Chronicles 28:20. The combination of physical courage with moral fidelity is the Deuteronomic ideal of kingship: external strength rooted in internal obedience.
3: wĕšāmartā ʾeṯ-mišmereṯ YHWH ʾĕlōheykā laḥăleḵeṯ biḏrāḵāyw lišmōr ḥuqqōṯāyw miṣwōṯāyw ûmišpāṭāyw wĕʿēḏwōṯāyw: 'Keep the charge of YHWH your God, walking in his ways, keeping his statutes, commandments, ordinances, and testimonies.' The fourfold law vocabulary — ḥuqqōṯ (statutes, practices), miṣwōṯ (commandments), mišpāṭîm (ordinances/judgments), ʿēḏwōṯ (testimonies) — is the full Deuteronomic law-formula that appears in Deuteronomy 4:45; 1 Kings 6:1; Ps 119 (all four terms throughout the psalm). This is the fullest possible expression of Torah faithfulness, covering every category of covenant obligation. The instruction is explicitly grounded in 'what is written in the Torah of Moses.'
4: lĕmaʿan yāqîm YHWH ʾeṯ-dĕḇārô ʾăšer dibbēr ʿālay lēʾmōr ʾim-yišmĕrû ḇāneyḵā ʾeṯ-darKām laḥăleḵeṯ lĕp̄ānay beʾĕmeṯ bĕḵol-lĕḇāḇām ûḇĕḵol-nap̄šām: 'That YHWH may establish his word that he spoke concerning me, saying, If your sons keep their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart and with all their soul, you shall not lack a man on the throne of Israel.' The conditional clause — ʾim-yišmĕrû ḇāneyḵā (if your sons keep) — reveals the conditionality of the Davidic covenant that is usually overlooked. The unconditional aspect is the dynasty's existence; the conditional aspect is its specific kings' continued possession of the throne. The formula bĕḵol-lĕḇāḇām ûḇĕḵol-nap̄šām (with all their heart and with all their soul) is the Shema formula (Deuteronomy 6:5), making obedience to Torah the explicit condition of covenant blessing.
5: wĕgam ʾattâ yāḏaʿtā ʾēṯ ʾăšer-ʿāśâ lî Yôʾāḇ ben-Ṣĕrûyâ ʾăšer ʿāśâ lišnê śārê ṣiḇĕʾōṯ yiśrāʾēl lĕʾAḇnēr ben-Nēr ûlĕʿAmāśāʾ ben-Yeṯer: 'You also know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me, what he did to the two commanders of the armies of Israel, to Abner son of Ner and to Amasa son of Jether.' The key phrase is wayyāśem dĕmê milḥāmâ bĕšālôm (he shed blood of war in peacetime): Joab killed Abner and Amasa not in battle but during peacetime negotiations and political transitions (2 Samuel 3:27; 1 Kings 20:10), making their deaths murders rather than legitimate warfare. Dam milḥāmâ bĕšālôm (blood of war in peace) is the legal definition: killing with war's violence during peace's terms = murder.
6: wĕʿāśîṯā kĕḥokmāṯeḵā wĕlōʾ-ṯôrēd śêḇāṯô bĕšālôm šĕʾôl: 'Act according to your wisdom and do not let his gray hair go down to Sheol in peace.' Ḵokmāṯeḵā (your wisdom) — the appeal to wisdom rather than specific instruction anticipates the entire Solomon narrative, whose theological center is wisdom (the gift of 1 Kings 3). David trusts Solomon's judgment to find the appropriate means for bringing Joab to account. Śêḇâ (gray head, old age) is a metonym for the person at the end of life — 'do not let his gray hair descend peacefully to Sheol' means: he should not die in his bed of old age, unpunished for his crimes.
7: ûlĕḇĕnê Barzillay haggĭlʿāḏî taʿăśeh ḥeseḏ wĕhāyû bĕʾōḵĕlê šulḥāneḵā: 'Deal loyally with the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite and let them be among those who eat at your table.' The instruction regarding Barzillai's sons is the positive counterpart to the negative instructions about Joab and Shimei. Ḥeseḏ (covenant loyalty, steadfast love) is the specific obligation: Barzillai had supported David during Absalom's rebellion (2 Samuel 17:27-29; 1 Kings 19:31-40), and this ḥeseḏ creates an ongoing obligation of reciprocal loyalty. The phrase 'eat at your table' (ʾāḵal bĕšulḥāneḵā) is the covenant-belonging idiom — the same formula used for Mephibosheth in 2 Samuel 9:7-13.
8: wĕhinnēh ʿimmĕkā Šimʿî ben-Gērāʾ ben-hayyĕmînî miBaḥûrîm: 'Behold, with you is Shimei son of Gera the Benjaminite from Bahurim.' Shimei cursed David during Absalom's rebellion (bĕyôm lĕktî maḥănāyim, 2 Samuel 16:5-8) — 'on the day I went to Mahanaim.' David spared him when returning victorious (2 Samuel 19:16-23), swearing ʾim-tāmûṯ (you shall not die) — but the oath was sworn by David, not by Solomon. David is technically not binding Solomon to inaction; he is transferring the obligation to Solomon's judgment (wĕʾattâ tēḏaʿ ʾēṯ ʾăšer taʿăśeh-lô, you know what you ought to do to him). David's oath protected Shimei during David's lifetime; Solomon's wisdom and justice now govern the matter.
9: wĕhāyā-dāmô ʿal-rōʾšô: 'His blood will be on his own head.' The blood-guilt formula dāmô ʿal-rōʾšô (his blood on his head) is the standard legal idiom for self-incurred capital guilt in which the person's own actions are the cause of their death (Joshua 2:19: Rahab's assurance that anyone who leaves her house = their blood on their own head; 1 Samuel 25:39: Nabal's blood on his own head). David is not commanding Solomon to kill Shimei arbitrarily but pronouncing that Shimei's past crimes (cursing the LORD's anointed = capital offense under Exodus 22:28) leave him with ongoing blood-guilt that will justify Solomon's action if and when he acts.
10: wayyiškaḇ Dāwîḏ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqqāḇēr bĕʿîr Dāwîḏ: 'David slept with his fathers and was buried in the City of David.' Wayyiškaḇ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw (he lay/slept with his fathers) is the standard death-formula for Israelite kings in 1-2 Kings — it will appear dozens of times. The burial in the ʿîr Dāwîḏ (City of David) is the royal burial location: the Davidic kings were buried in Jerusalem, in the royal necropolis on the original Jebusite hill. Acts 2:29 notes that David's tomb was known in Peter's day, confirming the long tradition of Davidic burial in Jerusalem.
11: wĕhāyāmîm ʾăšer mālaḵ Dāwîḏ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl ʾarbaʿîm šānâ: 'The days that David reigned over Israel were forty years: seven years in Hebron and thirty-three years in Jerusalem.' The 40-year reign formula — šeḇaʿ šānîm mālaḵ bĕḥeḇrôn ûšĕlōšîm wĕšālōš šānâ mālaḵ bîrûšālaim — is an exact summary of 2 Samuel 2:11 (7 years in Hebron) + 2 Samuel 5:4-5 (33 in Jerusalem = 40 total). The 40-year period is the standard generation-length in OT chronology (wilderness: 40 years; Saul: 40 years; David: 40 years; Solomon: 40 years), though the specific figure for David has corroboration in the detailed chronological data of 2 Samuel.
12: ûšĕlōmōh yāšaḇ ʿal-kissēʾ Dāwîḏ ʾāḇîw waṯiqqōn malKûṯô mĕʾōḏ: 'And Solomon sat on the throne of David his father, and his kingdom was firmly established greatly.' The verb kûn (Niphal, to be established/firm) is the root of the noun nākôn (established, secure). The Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12-16) used the same root: wĕhăkinnōṯî ʾeṯ-malkûṯô (I will establish his kingdom). Solomon's established kingdom is the initial fulfillment of the covenant promise — at least at the political level. The word mĕʾōḏ (greatly, exceedingly) emphasizes the completeness of the consolidation, which will be demonstrated in the elimination of the competing power centers (Adonijah, Joab, Abiathar, Shimei) in vv13-46.
13: wayyāḇōʾ ʾAḏōniyyāhû ben-Ḥaggîṯ ʾel-Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾēm šĕlōmōh: 'Adonijah son of Haggith came to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon.' Adonijah's approach to Bathsheba rather than directly to Solomon is a political strategy: if he can get the queen mother to intercede, the request gains implicit backing from someone with standing before the king. The queen mother (geḇîrâ) in the Israelite and Judahite court had a recognized institutional role — 1 Kings 15:13 (Asa removes Maacah from the geḇîrâ position) and 2 Kings 10:13 (the sons of Ahaziah going to greet the sons of the queen mother) both suggest real political influence.
14: wayyōʾmer lāh dāḇār šālôm lî waṯōʾmer šālôm: 'He said, I have something to say to you. She said, Speak.' The brief exchange is the formal request-to-speak protocol: the petitioner announces a desire to speak, the host invites. Bathsheba's simple šālôm (peace/well) is polite acceptance. She has no way to know at this point that the request is politically dangerous.
15: wayyōʾmer ʾattâ yāḏaʿtā kî-lî hāyĕṯâ hammmamlāḵâ wĕʿālay śāmû kol-yiśrāʾēl p̄ānêhem limĕlōḵ: 'You know that the kingdom was mine, and that all Israel set their faces on me to reign.' Adonijah's claim that the kingdom was 'his' is the first indicator of his continuing illegitimate ambition. His self-justification — 'all Israel set their faces on me' — invokes popular support as the ground for his claim. But the Deuteronomistic History consistently evaluates succession claims by divine authorization (prophetic oracle, anointing) rather than popular acclamation. Adonijah's appeal to popular sentiment rather than divine appointment reveals the deficiency in his claim.
16: wĕʿattâ šĕʾēlâ ʾaḥaṯ ʾānōḵî šōʾēl mēʾittāḵ ʾal-tĕḵabbî p̄ānāyiḵ: 'And now I am asking one request of you; do not refuse me.' The formal request structure — šĕʾēlâ ʾaḥaṯ (one petition) + ʾal-tĕḵabbî p̄ānāyiḵ (do not hide your face, i.e., do not refuse) — is the court petition formula. Bathsheba's daḇḇērî (speak!) accepts the petition for hearing. Adonijah has not yet revealed the content, but the elaborate setup signals the sensitivity of what he is about to request.
17: wayyōʾmer ʾimrî nā lišĕlōmōh hammelek kî lōʾ-yāšîḇ ʾeṯ-p̄ānāyiḵ wĕyitten-lî ʾeṯ-ʾAḇîšaḡ haššûnammîṯ lĕʾiššâ: 'He said, Please speak to King Solomon — for he will not refuse you — that he give me Abishag the Shunammite as wife.' The request for Abishag is politically explosive: under ANE succession law, the harem of a deceased or deposed king passed to his successor (cf. 2 Samuel 12:8; 1 Kings 16:21-22). Requesting one of the king's women — even if unconsummated — was equivalent to claiming succession rights. David 'did not know her' (1 Kings 1:4), but her status as the king's attendant was publicly known. Adonijah's request is therefore either deliberately provocative (a succession claim) or shows spectacular political naivety.
18: waṯōʾmer Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ṭôḇ ʾānōḵî ʾăḏabbēr ʿāleykā ʾel-hammelek: 'Bathsheba said, Very well, I will speak for you to the king.' Bathsheba's agreement to intercede does not necessarily indicate she understands the political implications of the request; she may be acting in good faith as an intermediary. Alternatively, she may understand perfectly and is allowing Adonijah to expose himself. The narrator does not specify her motivation, leaving the reader to evaluate based on the outcome: the petition proves self-destructive for Adonijah.
19: waṯāḇōʾ Baṯ-šeḇaʿ ʾel-hammelek šĕlōmōh lĕḏabbēr lô ʿal-ʾAḏōniyyāhû wayyāqom hammelek liqrāṯāh wayyišṯaḥû lāh: 'Bathsheba came to King Solomon to speak to him for Adonijah, and the king rose to meet her and bowed to her.' Solomon's reception of his mother — rising to meet her, bowing to her, providing a throne-seat at his right hand (kissēʾ lĕʾēm hammelek waṯēšeḇ lîmînô) — is the recognition of the queen-mother's institutional status. The geḇîrâ sitting at the king's right hand is the position of honor and authority in the court. This visual detail establishes the theological subtext: Bathsheba's request, given from this position of honor, should be persuasive.
20: waṯōʾmer šĕʾēlâ qĕṭannâ ʾaḥaṯ ʾānōḵî šōʾeleṯ mēʾittāḵ ʾal-tāšēḇ ʾeṯ-p̄ānāy: 'She said, I ask one small request of you; do not refuse me.' The self-deprecation qĕṭannâ (small, little) before the petition is court rhetoric — Bathsheba minimizes the request to make refusal seem unreasonable. Solomon's response — šăʾălî ʾimmi wĕlōʾ ʾāšîḇ ʾeṯ-p̄ānāyiḵ (Ask, my mother, for I will not refuse you) — is the royal grant of a boon before knowing its content, a standard literary motif (cf. Mark 6:23: Herod's rash promise to Herodias's daughter). The unconditional promise sets up the narrative tension: Solomon has committed before hearing.
21: waṯōʾmer yuttān ʾeṯ-ʾAḇîšaḡ haššûnammîṯ lĕʾAḏōniyyāhû ʾāḥîḵā lĕʾiššâ: 'She said, Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah your brother as wife.' The request is delivered straightforwardly. Bathsheba's presentation of Adonijah as 'your brother' (ʾāḥîḵā) may be an attempt to soften the request by invoking fraternal obligation. Solomon's reaction — explosive anger — demonstrates that he immediately sees the political danger that Bathsheba perhaps did not.
22: wayyaʿan hammmelek šĕlōmōh wayyōʾmer lĕʾimmô wĕlāmmâ ʾattî šōʾeleṯ ʾeṯ-ʾAḇîšaḡ haššûnammîṯ lĕʾAḏōniyyāhû ûšĕʾălî lô ʾeṯ-hammamlāḵâ: 'Solomon answered his mother, Why do you ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? Ask for him the kingdom as well!' Solomon's insight is rhetorical and sharp: ûšĕʾălî lô ʾeṯ-hammamlāḵâ (ask the kingdom for him too!) makes explicit what was implicit in the request. Because Adonijah is the next oldest son of David after the death of Amnon and Absalom, and because possession of the royal concubines/attendants was a succession symbol (2 Samuel 16:21-22), the request for Abishag is tantamount to a succession claim. Solomon has identified the political threat clearly.
23: wayyiššāḇaʿ hammmelek šĕlōmōh bĕYHWH lēʾmōr kōh yaʿăśeh-lî ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵōh yōsîp̄ kî bĕnap̄šô dibbēr ʾAḏōniyyāhû ʾeṯ-haddāḇār hazzeh: 'King Solomon swore by YHWH saying, God do so to me and more also if Adonijah has not spoken this word against his own life!' The oath formula kōh yaʿăśeh-lî ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵōh yōsîp̄ (God do so to me and more) is the standard Hebrew self-imprecation — calling down divine judgment on oneself if the statement is false. The phrase bĕnap̄šô dibbēr... ʾeṯ-haddāḇār hazzeh (against his own life he spoke this word) means: the request itself is Adonijah's execution warrant; he has condemned himself by the petition.
24: wĕʿattâ ḥay YHWH ʾăšer hĕḵînanî wayyōšîḇēnî ʿal-kissēʾ Dāwîḏ ʾāḇî wĕʾăšer ʿāśâ-lî ḇayiṯ kaʾăšer dibbēr kî hayyôm yûmāṯ ʾAḏōniyyāhû: 'As YHWH lives, who has established me and placed me on the throne of David my father and who has made me a house as he promised, today Adonijah shall be put to death.' The oath formula ḥay YHWH invokes the living God as guarantor. The relative clause establishes Solomon's theological ground: YHWH has established his throne and fulfilled his promise — this is covenant confirmation. Adonijah's attempt to undermine this YHWH-established order is therefore not merely political rebellion but theological provocation.
25: wayišlaḥ hammelek šĕlōmōh bĕyaḏ Bĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wayyippĕgaʿ-bô wayyāmōṯ: 'King Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and he struck him and he died.' Benaiah's role as executioner throughout 1 Kings 2 (Adonijah: v25; Joab: v34; Shimei: v46) establishes him as the instrument of the royal justice administration. The verb pāgaʿ (to strike, meet with fatal result) is used for the execution of Agag by Samuel (1 Samuel 15:32) and will be used again for Joab (v29). The brief, formulaic report — no detail of the execution, no lament — is the cold administrative register of the court chronicle.
26: ûlĕʾAḇyāṯār hakkōhēn ʾāmar hammelek ʿAnāṯôṯ lēḵ ʿal-śāḏeykā kî ʾîš māweṯ ʾattâ: 'To Abiathar the priest the king said, Go to Anathoth to your field, for you are a man of death.' Solomon's judgment on Abiathar is banishment rather than execution — three reasons are given for mercy: (1) he carried the Ark (nāśāʾtā ʾeṯ-ʾărôn ʾăḏōnāy YHWH), (2) he was afflicted in all that David was afflicted (ʾăšer hiṯʿannîṯā bĕḵōl ʾăšer-hiṯʿannâ ʾāḇî), (3) the fulfillment of prophecy requires him to live to see it (v27). The phrase ʾîš māweṯ ʾattâ (you are a man of death, i.e., you deserve death) acknowledges the treason while explaining why the expected sentence is commuted.
27: waygāreš šĕlōmōh ʾeṯ-ʾAḇyāṯār mĭhyôṯ kōhēn lĕYHWH lĕmallēʾ ʾeṯ-dĕḇar YHWH ʾăšer dibbēr ʿal-bêṯ ʿēlî bĕšîlōh: 'Solomon expelled Abiathar from being priest to YHWH, to fulfill the word of YHWH that he spoke concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh.' The explicit fulfillment formula — lĕmallēʾ ʾeṯ-dĕḇar YHWH (to fulfill the word of YHWH) — is one of the key theological moves in 1-2 Kings: events are interpreted as fulfillments of specific prophetic words. The 1 Samuel 2:31-33 prophecy against Eli's house (no old man in Eli's line; a faithful priest will come) is here declared fulfilled. Abiathar is the last of Eli's priestly line in royal service; his expulsion ends that dynasty.
28: wĕhaššĕmûʿâ bāʾâ ʿaḏ-Yôʾāḇ kî Yôʾāḇ nāṭâ ʾaḥărê ʾAḏōniyyāhû wĕʾaḥărê ʾAḇšālôm lōʾ nāṭâ: 'The news reached Joab — for Joab had turned after Adonijah, though after Absalom he had not turned.' The narrator's parenthetical — Joab followed Adonijah but not Absalom — is a calibrated observation: Joab's involvement in Absalom's rebellion was complex (he killed Absalom against David's orders) but he was never formally Absalom's partisan. His alignment with Adonijah was deliberate political choice. Nāṭâ ʾaḥărê (to turn/incline after) is the idiom for political allegiance.
29: wayyuggad lišĕlōmōh kî-nās Yôʾāḇ ʾel-ʾōhel YHWH wĕhinnēh ʾēṣel hammizbēaḥ: 'Solomon was told that Joab had fled to the tent of YHWH and was by the altar.' Joab's flight to the altar sanctuary parallels Adonijah's in 1 Kings 1:50. But Joab refuses to leave when commanded — lōʾ ʾāṣēʾ (I will not go out) — unlike Adonijah who came down from the altar when summoned (1 Kings 1:53). Joab's altar-clinging is more desperate and more politically astute: he knows that leaving the sanctuary = execution. Solomon's command to Benaiah — 'go, fall upon him' (lēḵ p̄ĕgaʿ-bô) even at the altar — reflects the Exodus 21:14 exception: premeditated murder forfeits altar asylum rights.
30: wayyāḇōʾ Bĕnāyāhû ʾel-ʾōhel YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw kōh-ʾāmar hammelek ṣēʾ wayyōʾmer lōʾ kî p̄ōh ʾāmûṯ: 'Benaiah came to the tent of YHWH and said to him, Thus says the king, Come out. He said, No, for here I will die.' Joab's declaration — p̄ōh ʾāmûṯ (here I will die) — is a refusal of the sanctuary's conditional protection and a claim that only the altar-space will protect him. It is also the last act of defiance of a man who has resisted royal authority throughout the David narrative. The confrontation between Benaiah and Joab plays out the entire tension between institutional military power (the gibbôrîm under Benaiah, loyal to David/Solomon) and the older military establishment (Joab, loyal only to his own judgment).
31: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw hammelek ʿăśēh kaʾăšer dibbēr wĕp̄ĕgaʿ-bô ûqĕḇartô: 'The king said to him, Do as he says; strike him and bury him.' Solomon's authorization for execution at the altar is based on Exodus 21:14: 'if a man willfully attacks another to kill him treacherously, you shall take him from my altar, that he may die.' Joab's murders of Abner and Amasa were precisely treacherous, premeditated killings — the category for which altar asylum is explicitly denied. The burial command (qĕḇartô) is a sign of minimal respect: Joab's body will not be left unburied, though his execution is just. Verse 34 records the burial 'in his own house in the wilderness.'
32: wĕhēšîḇ YHWH ʾeṯ-dāmô ʿal-rōʾšô: 'YHWH will return his blood on his head.' The theological interpretation of Joab's execution is explicitly given: this is YHWH's justice for the blood Joab shed. The formula dāmô ʿal-rōʾšô (blood on his head) — repeated in vv33, 37, 44 — is the covenant-justice formula of blood-guilt falling on the guilty party. Solomon is not merely executing a political rival; he is administering the covenant law of blood-guilt. The deaths of Abner and Amasa (and implicitly Uriah through Joab's complicity) are being legally addressed by the new king's justice.
33: wĕhāyâ dĕmêhem bĕrōʾš Yôʾāḇ ûbĕrōʾš zarʿô lĕʿōlām: 'Their blood shall return upon the head of Joab and upon the head of his descendants forever.' The extension of blood-guilt to Joab's descendants (zarʿô lĕʿōlām) echoes the covenant curse-structure of Deut 28 — sins have generational consequences. The contrasting blessing — wĕlĕḏāwîḏ ûlĕzarʿô ûlĕḇêṯô ûlĕkissĕʾô yihyeh šālôm ʿaḏ-ʿôlām mēʿim YHWH (to David and his seed and his house and his throne there will be peace forever from YHWH) — is the covenant counterpart: the Davidic dynasty inherits perpetual peace while Joab's line inherits perpetual blood-guilt. The covenant binary is applied to specific individuals.
34: wayyaʿal Bĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wayyippĕgaʿ-bô wayyĕmîṯēhû wayyiqqāḇēr bĕḇêṯô bammiḏbār: 'Benaiah son of Jehoiada went up and attacked him and killed him, and he was buried in his own house in the wilderness.' The execution at the altar — wayyaʿal (went up, i.e., into the sanctuary) — is the enforcement of Exodus 21:14. The burial notation (bĕḇêṯô bammiḏbār, in his house in the wilderness) suggests Joab's personal property was in the Judean wilderness, fitting for a warrior of his background. The execution of Joab completes the narrative arc begun in 2 Sam 3 (Abner's murder) — a three-book span before blood-guilt is finally addressed.
35: wayyiṯṭēn hammelek ʾeṯ-Bĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ taḥtāyw ʿal-haṣṣāḇāʾ wĕʾeṯ-Ṣāḏôq hakkōhēn nāṯan hammelek taḥaṯ ʾAḇyāṯār: 'The king appointed Benaiah son of Jehoiada in his (Joab's) place over the army, and Zadok the priest the king appointed in place of Abiathar.' The succession of appointments — Benaiah for Joab, Zadok for Abiathar — completes the institutional restructuring. Both positions now belong to men who were loyal to Solomon from the beginning (Benaiah in 1 Kings 1:10; Zadok in 1 Kings 1:8). The Zadokite priestly line that will serve the Jerusalem temple through the exile is now established as the sole priestly dynasty, with Abiathar (Eli's last descendant) removed. Ezekiel's vision specifies the Zadokite priests as the legitimate priests of the eschatological temple (Ezekiel 44:15-16).
36: wayišlaḥ hammelek wayyiqrāʾ-lišimʿî wayyōʾmer lô bĕnēh lĕḵā ḇayiṯ bîrûšālaim wĕyāšaḇtā šām: 'The king sent and called Shimei and said to him, Build yourself a house in Jerusalem and dwell there.' Solomon's terms for Shimei are precise: house arrest in Jerusalem, with the explicit boundary condition — bĕyôm ṣēʾṯĕkā wĕʿāḇartā ʾeṯ-naḥal qiḏrôn yāḏōaʿ tēḏaʿ kî môṯ tāmûṯ (on the day you go out and cross the Wadi Kidron, know for certain that you shall die). The Kidron Valley is the boundary of Jerusalem to the east; crossing it toward Bethlehem or the Jordan valley = unauthorized departure. The arrangement is a supervised mercy: Shimei lives, but under covenant terms whose violation will trigger death.
37: wĕhāyâ bĕyôm ṣēʾṯĕkā wĕʿāḇartā ʾeṯ-naḥal qiḏrôn yāḏōaʿ tēḏaʿ kî môṯ tāmûṯ dāmeḵā yihyeh bĕrōʾšeḵā: 'On the day you go out and cross the Wadi Kidron, know certainly that you shall die; your blood will be on your own head.' The double infinitive môṯ tāmûṯ (dying you shall die = you shall surely die) is the emphatic death-sentence formula of Genesis 2:17 and throughout the legal and narrative tradition. The blood-guilt formula (dāmeḵā yihyeh bĕrōʾšeḵā) makes the death self-incurred: Solomon is not threatening arbitrary execution but setting a boundary whose crossing = Shimei's own choice to forfeit the covenant of mercy. Shimei accepts the terms in v38.
38: wayyōʾmer Šimʿî lammmelek ṭôḇ haddāḇār kĕʾăšer dibbēr ʾăḏōnî hammelek kēn yaʿăśeh ʿaḇdĕkā: 'Shimei said to the king, Good is the word; as my lord the king has said, so your servant will do.' Shimei's acceptance of the covenant terms is formal and explicit. Ṭôḇ haddāḇār (the word is good) is covenant ratification language — the party assents to the terms offered. The self-designation ʿaḇdĕkā (your servant) is the formula of submission. The narrator notes he dwelt in Jerusalem yamîm rabbîm (many days) — time passes, the boundary condition seems distant, and then Shimei violates it.
39: wayĕhî miqṣēh šālōš šānîm wayyibrĕḥû šnê ʾăḇāḏîm lĕšimʿî ʾel-ʾĀḵîš ben-Maʿăḵâ melek Gaṯ: 'At the end of three years, two slaves of Shimei ran away to Achish son of Maacah king of Gath.' The three-year gap establishes that the covenant terms had been stable and observed before the crisis. The slaves' flight to Gath — Philistine territory, beyond the Kidron boundary — creates Shimei's dilemma: retrieve the slaves = violate the Kidron boundary = death; leave the slaves = loss of property. Shimei chooses to retrieve his slaves (wayyaqom Šimʿî wayyaḥăbōš ʾeṯ-ḥămōrô wayyēleḵ Gaṯâ), demonstrating that his material interests override the covenant terms he accepted.
40: wayyāqom Šimʿî wayyaḥăbōš ʾeṯ-ḥămōrô wayyēleḵ Gaṯâ ʾel-ʾĀḵîš lĕḇaqēš ʾeṯ-ʿăḇāḏāyw: 'Shimei rose and saddled his donkey and went to Gath to Achish to seek his slaves.' The saddling of the donkey is the preparation-for-journey formula; the destination (Gath, Philistine territory) is unambiguous. The detail that Shimei went to retrieve slaves (ʿăḇāḏāyw) is the narrative's explanation of why he crossed the boundary — not a political escape attempt but a property recovery operation. Nevertheless, the violation is the violation: the specific boundary and condition were set by covenant, and Shimei crossed it.
41: wayyuggad lišĕlōmōh kî hālaḵ Šimʿî mîrûšālaim Gaṯâ wayyāšāḇ: 'Solomon was told that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath and returned.' The report reaching Solomon — including both the departure and the return — triggers the execution. The covenant terms were explicit and Shimei had formally accepted them. Solomon's response follows the same legal logic as his response to Adonijah: the violation of the covenant term is self-incurred death (dāmeḵā bĕrōʾšeḵā). The legal precision of the Solomon narrative is characteristic: each execution is preceded by a specific covenant violation or crime.
42: wayišlaḥ hammelek wayyiqrāʾ lĕšimʿî wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw hălōʾ hišbaʿtîḵā ḇaYHWH: 'The king sent and called Shimei and said to him, Did I not make you swear by YHWH?' Solomon's confrontation with Shimei rehearses the covenant terms: the oath sworn by YHWH (hišbaʿtîḵā ḇaYHWH), the explicit warning (ḥāʿēḏ bĕkā lēʾmōr, I warned you), and the accepted terms (môṯ tāmûṯ). The phrase waṯōʾmer ʾēlay ṭôḇ haddāḇār šāmaʿtî (you said to me, Good is the word; I have heard) quotes back Shimei's own acceptance of the covenant in v38. The evidence against him is his own words.
43: wĕlāmmâ lōʾ šāmartā ʾēṯ šĕḇûʿaṯ YHWH wĕʾeṯ-hammiṣwâ ʾăšer ṣiwwîṯî ʿāleykā: 'Why then have you not kept the oath of YHWH and the commandment I laid on you?' The language — šĕḇûʿaṯ YHWH (the oath of YHWH) and miṣwâ (commandment) — elevates the covenant terms from a political arrangement to a religious obligation. The oath sworn by YHWH as guarantor makes its violation an offense against YHWH, not merely against Solomon. This legal framing is consistent with the theological logic of 1-2 Kings: political covenants sworn by YHWH are covenant-theological obligations, not merely civil contracts.
44: wayyōʾmer hammelek ʾel-Šimʿî ʾattâ yāḏaʿtā ʾēṯ kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer yāḏaʿ lĕḇāḇĕkā ʾăšer ʿāśîṯā lĕḏāwîḏ ʾāḇî: 'The king said to Shimei, You know all the evil that your heart knows that you did to David my father.' Solomon's final statement before the execution invokes Shimei's prior crimes: the cursing of David during Absalom's rebellion (2 Samuel 16:5-8), which Shimei knows in his own heart. Yāḏaʿ lĕḇāḇĕkā (your heart knows) — inner knowledge of guilt without external evidence required. The formula wĕhēšîḇ YHWH ʾeṯ-rāʿāṯĕkā bĕrōʾšeḵā (YHWH has returned your evil on your own head) is the divine-justice formula: YHWH administers the blood-guilt through the covenant community's judicial process.
45: wĕhammelek šĕlōmōh bārûḵ ûkissēʾ Dāwîḏ yihyeh nāḵôn lip̄nê YHWH ʿaḏ-ʿôlām: 'King Solomon shall be blessed, and the throne of David shall be established before YHWH forever.' The chapter's theological climax inverts the blood-guilt formula: Shimei's evil falls on Shimei's head; Solomon's blessing is confirmed; David's throne is established before YHWH forever. Lip̄nê YHWH ʿaḏ-ʿôlām (before YHWH forever) is the Davidic covenant language of 2 Samuel 7:16 — the throne established forever. The chapter ends not with political consolidation as its primary statement but with the fulfillment of the covenant promise.
46: wayyĕṣaw hammelek ʾeṯ-Bĕnāyāhû ben-Yĕhôyāḏāʿ wayyēṣēʾ wayyippĕgaʿ-bô wayyāmōṯ ûmamlaḵâ nāḵônâ bĕyaḏ šĕlōmōh: 'The king commanded Benaiah son of Jehoiada and he went out and struck him and he died. And the kingdom was established in Solomon's hand.' The closing formula — mamlaḵâ nāḵônâ bĕyaḏ šĕlōmōh (the kingdom was established in Solomon's hand) — echoes the coronation statement of 1 Kings 2:12 (waṯiqqōn malKûṯô mĕʾōḏ) to form a chiastic bracket around the entire consolidation narrative of vv13-46. The root kûn (to be established, firm) is the Davidic covenant's establishment-word (2 Samuel 7:12, 16); its application to Solomon's consolidated reign confirms that the covenant promise is now institutionally operative. The kingdom is 'in Solomon's hand' — not merely in name but in political reality.
